culture_description: The Ahtna sky culture is the result of fieldwork with Northern Dene peoples, investigating how they conceptualize and integrate the sky into systems of knowledge, practices, cosmology, and spirituality. Characteristic is a principal constellation: the incarnated spirit of a Traveler figure who circled the world in Distant Time.
english_names: That Which Moves Over Us; That [Human] Which Moves Over Us; That Which Moves Over the Earth; That Which Moves in a Circle Following Us; The One Who Went Around the Edge of the World; The One Who Went Around the Edge of the Water/Ocean; The One Who Leads Us Ahead; Smart Young Man
native_names: Nek'eltaeni; Nek'eltaenen; Nen'keltaeni; Nek'e Nekeghaltaexi; Yabaaghe Tezyaann; Yabaaghe Tuu Teeshyaay; Netseh Tełyaanen; Ciił Hywaa
certainty: Most stars are identified, and a pictograph is drawn in the source [cannon2021northerndene]. The line figure here follows the star path of each body part listed, connecting them. His kidney is an unidentified star near the cup of the Big Dipper (not included here). His right hand is 'two unidentified stars in Cassiopeia': the two closest stars are added here. The eyes (bet Tau, iot Aur) and heart (27 Lyn) are unconnected. It is not clear whether the abdomen should be delineated from the head with a neckline (bet Aur, alf Gem); that line is drawn here.
constellation_description: This is a meta-, whole-sky constellation; the individual body parts are explicitly separated in a table as asterisms. In local tradition, Nek’eltaeni was the first being to appear among the stars. Before going to the sky, he was the culture hero and Traveler figure known as Yabaaghe Tezyaann ('the one who went around the edge of the world') (and other similar names in Ahtna dialects), who transformed an archaic animal people into the present-day distinct animal species, and established peace and order on earth. He uses medicine power and is a model of compassion. Nek’eltaeni is a large man-animal or therianthrope figure composed of at least 18 asterisms named using body part terms. He wears a backpack (native: ughaele') and holds a walking stick/weapon/medicine staff (native: utets) in his left hand. The Milky Way is his trail (native: utene). His representation as a therianthrope or shapeshifter recalls ancient time when humans and animals were one.

constellation_description: Orion's belt. The stars were used for timing seasonal hunting activities.

culture_description: Anuta is a small island in the SE of Solomon Islands in Melanesia, inhabited by a culturally Polynesian society. As for most Oceanic seafarers, the stars are the most important tool for long-range navigation. The major guiding stars and constellations are known as carriers (native: kaavenga), and have proper names. A star path or sequence of stars (native: kau panonga porau) is followed from one island to the next. This sky culture has 11 constellation line figures. Two other groups of stars are mentioned in the source, but are not identifiable. Culturally significant themes reflected in Anutan constellations include birds, food, fish, and equipment used to procure or prepare these items. Many of these constellations are identified by similar names on other islands. The source draws each constellation with both stars and lines, and marks the Western constellation where this is placed. However, it only identifies the major stars by name, so marking the minor ones was guesswork based on the author's drawing.
certainty: Identified as part of Grus, but (based on the shape) doesn't use the brightest star alf Gru. No star is named. A subset of five stars are drawn in the source, taken here to be the brightest five besides alf Gru.
constellation_description: Bamboo is used to make fishing poles, sailing masts, yards, booms, and outrigger supports.

constellation_description: The dominant constellation for Anutans. It can be seen in all seasons and has practical navigational utility. From one wing tip to the other, it stretches across a quarter of the sky. Birds have symbolic prominence. Canoes are likened to birds, the sky is dominated by the Bird, and also the voyagers are metaphorically depicted as birds in poetry and song. In keeping with the theme of voyaging and navigation, the bird has a masculine identity, is an instigator of activity, with the freedom to move at will.

constellation_description: A group of men, women and children who are dancing in an oval, drunk, and playing the bass drum. The stars outside this oval are outsiders who are not allowed to join the dance. Dances interrupted by drinking have been widely used by this culture. They are started by the men; this constellation seems to represent an advanced state of the dance where there is no longer a separation of the sexes.

place: 1921 informants originally from SE Bolivia; interviewed in Ledesma and Orán, north-west Argentina, where indigenous tribes worked during the harvest
certainty: A line figure of 9+3 stars is drawn, and the stars are identified, but two of the minor ones have obsolete names. They (G Sco, HD 153613) are identified based on position.
constellation_description: A corral or fenced area, intended for the cultivation of plants, including corn. The center of this corral is the abode of the evil deity Aguará-Tunpa, the fox-god (the second-most powerful divinity of the Chiriguanos, always in struggle with the other, Aña Tunpa). However, the deity himself cannot be seen in this constellation. The stars are the demarcating posts of the great corral. In the center, three stars represent the Ahuala Süpei shovel with which he works: this heavenly shovel cleans the field and heals the corn by itself.

place: 1921 informants originally from SE Bolivia; interviewed in Ledesma and Orán, north-west Argentina, where indigenous tribes worked during the harvest
certainty: A line figure of 8 stars is drawn, and the stars are identified, except the most minor one has obsolete names. It (HD 172910) is identified based on position.
constellation_description: A corral of the tapir-god, a divinity analogous to the fox-god (Aguará Tunpa) who also has his corral in the sky, neighbouring this corral. The deity himself cannot be seen in this constellation, either. The Moreví Tunpa appears rarely in the local mythology, but it is likely that an interpretation of the Milky Way as the Path of the Tapir also refers to the Moreví Tunpa.

place: 1921 informants originally from SE Bolivia; interviewed in Ledesma and Orán, north-west Argentina, where indigenous tribes worked during the harvest
certainty: A line figure of 10 stars is drawn, but some stars are not identified. They are, however, clear from their relative position to the ones who are (the major stars in Cru and Cen). The line of the necklace (alf-bet Cen) is linked to the rest of the figure from a middle point on the line; this is not reproduced here: bet Cen (the closest star) is linked to the rest of the figure.
constellation_description: This rhea head has a notable peculiarity: while a real rhea is distinguished by a 'necklace' of dark feathers located at the base of the neck, this celestial one has the same ornament, but made of two splendid stars, alf and bet Cen. This necklace is called Yandu Pó'iéra. The beak is alf Cru, and the eyes bet and del Cru. In other cultures, IAU Cru forms the foot of the rhea.

place: Seven Bajara villages (south of Nagpur, India): Dapora, Phulumari, Katarwadi, Kasola, Chinchkhed, Palaitanda, Nagrala
culture_description: The Banjara are an ancient tribe mostly spread in Central and Western India (and connected genetically with the gypsies of Europe), speaking an Austro-Asiatic language. The population size is over 5 million. They are co-located with two other tribes: the Kolam and the Gonds, so these traditions are similar (the source [vahia2014banjaraskolam] provides a comparison). The primary profession of the Banjara used to be trade, with large-scale movement of goods over long distances. Their services were replaced by railways about one hundred years before this documentation. Since then, they have begun a settled, agricultural existence. Their astronomy is basic, and was not used for travel, not for predicting events. The sky objects identified vary between villages, with Saptarshi and the Pleiades the only almost universally recognised constellations among villages. Only one lined constellation (Saptarshi) has the stars identified. Three others are named but only their sky region is identified: Canis Major is called Medi and is a harvest-processing scene, a part of Orion is a deer called Halni, Halini or Harini, and a part of Taurus is a dove (called Kamedi).
english_names: Golden Cot Pursued by Three Thieves
certainty: The lining of the 4-star polygon is certain, but it is less clear whether the remaining 3 stars (a procession of people) should be lined. There are alternative semantics, since this is a group scene (two objects: a cot and a procession of three humans).
constellation_description: Saptarshi is divided into the four stars of the polygon that form the death bed (called Jamakhat or Jamkhat or Yamakhat), with a procession of three people following the bed.

place: Northern Great Plains of North America
culture_description: The Blackfoot is an Algonquian-speaking tribe living in the northern Great Plains of North America. This sky culture has 4 constellation line figures, and names for the Milky Way, the Pleiades, the Orion Nebula, three planets and three stars. This data is a transcript of the broad survey of North-American astronomical traditions in the book Stars of the first people: Native American star myths and constellations [miller1997stars] (from page 243). The basic information is not collected by this recent author. It is attributed to anthropologists from the early 1900s: Mythology of the Blackfoot Indians published in 1908 [wissler1908mythology], and `The old north trail, or, Life, legends and religion of the Blackfeet Indians` published in 1910 [mcclintock1910old]. Source [miller1997stars] instead collects the data and draws the constellations explicitly, based on this early information. According to the sources, the Blackfoot often featured the Big Dipper, the Pleiades, and the Morning Star on their tipis, and believe that when people die, their spirits go into the sky and become stars, ever increasing the number of stars in the heavens. The sources include many associated star stories (not transcribed here), and mostly English names. Another constellation called Hand in the Sky has been documented, but was not identified (so is not in this dataset). It could be another name for the Spider Man's Fingers, or another constellation entirely. An associated story also explains a particular tipi design.
certainty: The source doesn't explicitly provide the constellation lines, but identifies it with the modern Big Dipper, as a bowl with a handle.
constellation_description: Represents siblings who escape to the sky when being chased by a bear. The sister is the small star Alcor in this version. In a second version of this constellation (not included separately here), the youngest brother becomes the North Star, and the sister (who used to carry him) is the pointer star at the lip of the bowl. This constellation was used for time-keeping before the introduction of clocks.

place: Aldeia Indígena Garças, State of Mato Grosso, Brazil (the 2nd largest Bororo village, where most data came from)
culture_description: Only 700 culturally viable Bororo survive, in 9 villages. Their myths were documented by Claude Lévi-Strauss: the Toribugu myth (with astronomical symbolism) was the key myth in his Mythologiques. The Bororo have a rather large body of astronomical information (they recognise and name a large number of stars and constellations). In [fabian1992space], the astronomical information is covered in Ch. 7 (p. 125-153) and Appendix A-B; the constellations identified are explicitly drawn with lines on two sky charts. The Bororo men have a nightly practice in June (when the skies are clear) of stellar observations at sunset (documented also in 1936 by Claude Lévi-Strauss). The stars provide a way to mark the flow of time, and integrate the Bororo social and ecological cycles. Observations are made on the relationships between stars and other stars, or fixed points on the celestial sphere (such as the zenith). In the Bororo myth on the creation of the stars, the stars are children which escaped from earth, because their mothers were attempting to keep them in an infantile state by not feeding them corn. (There is a similar Gê myth.) The Bororo observe the stars' regular cycles, and describe them as beautiful: not only because they are colourful and bright, but also because of their regularity, which expresses the essence of being for the Bororo, who see themselves as socially ordered (there is a structural relationship between the Bororo astronomy and society, in that each is defined with respect to the other). There are 16 identified light constellations, including 4 turtles. Other constellations were named but not identified: Cayman (Uwai), and Large-Antlered Atubo Deer (Atubo). As is common among indigenous South Americans, the Bororo also recognise certain dark areas of the Milky Way as constellations. Pari (The Rhea, an ostrich-like bird) is a part-light and part-dark constellation, with the dark a large black patch of the Milky Way It is one of the most popular Bororo constellations. The bird is described as either sleeping or running, despite the bird's leg and foot extending alongside its neck and head. (This inconsistency is not explained.) There are also two variants of Mortar and Pestle (Kaia and Kaibore), black parts of the Milky Way either near Sirius, or near the tail of IAU Sco. there is also a light cloud constellation: Pari Kigadurewu (White Rhea), a large light cloud of the Milky Way. This is said to resemble the black rhea, Pari, and be 'below' it---but was not identified. This data should be considered incomplete (surviving remnants), since stellar observations are related to the aboriginal lifestyle, which is fading. Accurate identification of stars and constellations is difficult to obtain, with the young Bororo ignorant of the names of all but the most prominent sky objects.
certainty: Certain. Turtles have marked significance for the Bororo. The identification of the four turtle constellations is coherent acc. to the field research (they follow each other at 4-hour intervals with an alternation of the species, thus bracketing the night time).
constellation_description: This is the pincers of IAU Sco.

culture_description: The Bugis are an ethnic group on the island of Sulawesi in Indonesia, with a strong seafaring and trading tradition. This sky culture has 12 constellation line figures (of which two variants overlap in the Big Dipper region, and another two variants overlap in the Orion region). It also has names for four stars and five other sky objects (the Pleiades, the Coalsack Nebula, the Magellanic Cloud(s), the Milky Way, and Venus). Many of the constellations reflect the seafaring tradition; the others are of agricultural origin. This sky culture is sourced from [orchiston2021seasia] (Section 24.4.1.3 Bugis Asterisms on page 706), which provides a unified drawing of Bugis constellations from prior sources. These sources (one from 1999, one from 1987) had overlapping but not identical data. Most stars are identified, and most constellations lines are drawn, so can be considered certain unless specified otherwise. The Mandar sky culture in [orchiston2021mandar] is related (they are neighbouring ethnic groups on the island of Sulawesi) and also overlaps in information with [orchiston2021seasia]. Table 18.6 in [orchiston2021mandar] (page 562) compares the star names of the Mandar and Bugis (for Bugis, based on a source from 1999); the asterisms are similar, both in name and in form. Source [orchiston2021mandar] appears to have mistakes in star identification and typos in the native Bugis accents; they are corrected based on [orchiston2021seasia].

culture_description: Charles Dien was a French cartographer, engraver, and publisher. His 'Uranographie dressée sous l'inspection de M. Bouvard, astronome par Charles Dien' is a very early European lined star chart, successor to A. Ruelle's 'Nouvelle Uranographie' (although it is not known whether it was influenced by Ruelle's). It has the same chart style, but bears no strong similarity in constellation lines to Ruelle's line figures. Obsolete constellations are included (Cerberus, Musca Borealis, Antinoüs, Taurus Poniatovii). There are 75 lined constellations, plus 25 variants, plus 2 unlined (Antlia, Microscopium). place: 15 Gond villages around the Nagpur region in the states of Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh (India): Karambi, Nimni, Matharjun, Kesalapur, Kharmat, Wamanpalli, Khadaki, Yelodi, Zashinagar, Mohagaon, Kaweli, Chalisbodi, Kopariya, Sailakota, Lodha
culture_description: The Gond are one of the most ancient Indian tribes, dated to AD 890, but with roots going back to a pre-Dravidian arrival in south India around 2000 BC. Genetically they are a mix of Dravidian and Austro-Asian populations. The population size is 4-5 million in central India, with the core in the Kalahadi region of Orissa, and are co-located with the Kolam and Banjara tribes, so these traditions are similar (the source [vahia2014banjaraskolam] provides a comparison). They have farming traditions. They used astronomy for various purposes, from daily and annual time-keeping to projecting their life in the skies, and cosmogony; they have not used it for navigation. The source [vahia2013gond] identifies and names the stars on star charts, but the line figures on the same chart are Western, so are uncertain. General Gond names for stars are: Sukum, Sukir, Huko, Tara, or the adjective Lagni (bright). There are ten lined constellations, one star group, and names for Polaris, the Pleiades, the Milky Way, and Venus. Only the latter three, and the sky regions UMa and Ori are recognised in most villages.
english_names: Golden Cot Pursued by Three Thieves
certainty: The lining of the three thieves is uncertain; since they are named not as individuals, but as a group, they are lined together.
constellation_description: The 4 stars are Katul, a cot with four legs made of precious metals: gold, silver, inferior silver and copper. The last 3 stars are called Kalher or Kalir and represent three thieves who want to steal the cot when the old lady falls asleep. Hence the old lady never sleeps, indicating that the constellation never sets. Saptarshi is the primary reference point from which all constellations are located.

certainty: The lining is not certain. Tipan means plough in a newer source [vahia2014banjaraskolam] (Tipan or Trivpate are the same name in the co-located Kolam culture, denoting a plough) but is not called a plough in the primary Gond source [vahia2013gond]. The population size is 4-5 million in central India, with the core in the Kalahadi region of Orissa, and are co-located with the Kolam and Banjara tribes, so these traditions are similar (the source [vahia2014banjaraskolam] provides a comparison). They have farming traditions. They used astronomy for various purposes, from daily and annual time-keeping to projecting their life in the skies, and cosmogony; they have not used it for navigation. The source [vahia2013gond] identifies and names the stars on star charts, but the line figures on the same chart are Western, so are uncertain. General Gond names for stars are: Sukum, Sukir, Huko, Tara, or the adjective Lagni (bright). There are ten lined constellations, one star group, and names for Polaris, the Pleiades, the Milky Way, and Venus. Only the latter three, and the sky regions UMa and Ori are recognised in most villages.
certainty: Detailed identification of the stars and their role in the constellation, although this constellation is identified in only one village. Lines are not drawn by the source; here, they follow the description of the body part, forming a realistic stick figure.
constellation_description: The body of a dead person (CON Gond 012) supported by single stars: 4 pall bearers in Leo (at the extremities of the body), 4 unconnected criers in Leo, 1 unconnected carrier of funeral fire in Leo, and stars in Virgo (unspecified, so not included) which form the funeral procession. The procession moves from west to east. The Gond identify not only the body, but also the complete funeral procession of stars in order: Duf (the drum bearer), Shika (the procession leader), Murda (the dead body) and a group of 4 Ladavya (weeping women). This star combination is to the north (east?) of Scorpius (whose bite causes the Murda) and extends across the sky.

culture_description: The Gwich'in sky culture is the result of fieldwork with Northern Dene peoples, investigating how they conceptualize and integrate the sky into systems of knowledge, practices, cosmology, and spirituality. Characteristic is a principal constellation: the incarnated spirit of a Traveler figure who circled the world in Distant Time. A third constellation is known but was not shared with the researcher: a malevolent tiger-like being, 'the antithesis of everything that Yahdii was and continues to be' [cannon2021northerndene].
certainty: Most stars are identified, and a pictograph is drawn in the source [cannon2021northerndene]. The line figure here follows the star path of each body part listed, connecting them. The stars are well-corroborated by several people. Legs and arms and a part of the cane are not identified, so are not represented by stars here (they are only crossed by lines). Star eta Leo from the cane is skipped here (it creates an angle that does not match the pictograph). It is not clear whether the abdomen should be delineated from the head with a neckline (bet Aur, alf Gem); that line is included here.
constellation_description: Yahdii spans more than 143 degrees across the sky and is a meta-, whole-sky constellation; the individual body parts are explicitly separated in a table as at least 16 asterisms. Best observable between late January and March in Alaska's early evening sky. Yahdii wears a single-strapped bag (native: va’ǫhtsùu) on his left side and holds a walking stick/spear/medicine staff (vatòo) in his left hand. The Milky Way (native: vatąįį) is his snow-packed trail that circles the world. He is crouched on all fours facing down over earth in a protective position. The low- altitude asterisms near the horizon (head, hands, feet, stick) are considered to be located closest to earth, while the stars near in the center of the sky (body, heart, tail) are the farthest away. The apparent rotation of stars through the sky is referred to as 'Yahdii is walking' (native: Yahdii ahaa) or 'Yahdii moves/travels over the span' (native: Yahdii geelee nideekhaa). Although described as part human and part animal, the specific type of animal is personal knowledge aligned with the individual's primary animal helper, acquired through dreams or the vision quest. The dim 'red star' depicting Yahdii’s heart (27 Lyn) is his soul or spirit. Seeing this faint star is equivalent to knowing his soul, or having spiritual awareness. The eyes (bet Tau, iot Aur) and heart are unconnected.

certainty: Certain.
constellation_description: A linear constellation of three morning stars which appear in sequence on the northeast horizon from mid-October to early December. They are used to determine the timing of subsistence activities at dawn. They are also regarded as light and untethered spirits ascending to heaven. One person explained that Yahdii walks each night until he sees Yeedàak Gahàajil, so there is conceptual unity between these two constellations.

constellation_description: This represents a ceremonial object used in dance ceremonies: a baton with a red feather at one end, symbolising fire. This may have pre-colonial origins: the same constellation is also Aztec, called The New Fire (mamalhuaztli). A newwer meaning is that of the candelabra: a triangular light lit in church.

culture_description: 48 of the 88 IAU constellations are consolidated by ancient Greeks from the originals of the ancient Babylonian, Egyptian and Assyrian. These were recorded in Ptolemy’s Almagest. Between 16th-17th c. AD, European astronomers added new discoveries made by the Europeans who explored the southern hemisphere. Eugène Delporte originally listed the 88 modern constellations on behalf of the IAU Commission 3 (Astronomical Notations), in 'Délimitation scientifique des constellations' (Delporte, 1930). In star maps it is common to mark line figures. However, the IAU defines a constellation by its boundary (in sky coordinates). Alan MacRobert's constellation patterns, drawn in green in the charts on the IAU website, were influenced by those of H. A. Rey (1952), but in many cases were adjusted to preserve earlier traditions. Constellations Mic and Men are not listed here: they don't have main stars nor lines marked. Exceptionally, the Latin constellation names also form the English gloss, since they are in common use in English, and a translation would be less precise.

The Iroquois Confederacy, centered in the current New York State, consists of five tribes which made an alliance between 1400-1600 (the Seneca, Cayuga, Onondaga, Oneida, and Mohawk) and the Tuscarora, which joined in the early 1700s. They live in longhouses. Men hunted and fished, women planted and gathered. The stars are used to tell time, and (the North Star) as navigational marker. N. van Wassenaer, who wrote on the settlement of New Netherland in the early 1600s, noted that the Iroquois women were the most experienced stargazers, and knew the constellations well (but does not explain why). The source [miller1997stars] covers the Iroquois on pages 43-54, referencing earlier ethnographic sources from 1883-1922. It identifies a bear-and-hunters constellation (in two variants), locating it against Western ones. Another constellation is mentioned in a myth (1883), but is not identified (The Old Man). In another myth (1922), the Sky Woman made the sun, moon, and stars, forming the constellations: They Are Pursuing the Bear (CON Iroquois 001-003 located in the Big Dipper), It Brings the Day (Venus), They Are Dancing (Pleiades), She Is Sitting (located in IAU Ori, but without star identification), Beaver that Spreads its Skin (located in IAU UMi, but without star identification), The Group Visible (not located). Only three of the constellations identified have line figures.
constellation_description: In a story, a stone monster attacks a group of hunters. Only 3 survive (CON Iroquois 002). The bear is the bowl. All are carried to the sky. In the fall, the arrows pierce the bear's skin, making the sky red. In another story, a huge snow bear is eating the winter game of the Iroquois, so 3 brothers (CON Iroquois 002) chase the bear (the bowl) into the sky. The bear weaves an invisible net that it casts over the skies, and hides under it to rest (identified with CrB). When they reach the sleeping bear, the bear pushes the hunters away in the sky. The Iroquois (known as great runners) keep running into the sky, following the bear, who eludes them.

culture_description: Java has an agrarian society, cultivating rice. The Pleiades are the main time-keeper to determine cultivation times, followed by Orion. The astronomical traditional are similar to those of the other the ethnic groups of Java (Sunda, Bugis and Makassar) and other related groups in the subregion.
certainty: Identified and drawn in the source [van1885astronomie]. One star (marked 'c' in the source [van1885astronomie]) is replaced with sig Ori, which is the largest star in the area.
constellation_description: The Orion belt is the plough, and bet plus kap Ori are the oxen pulling the plough [van1885astronomie]. When the natives see the celestial plough rising in the east at dawn in an upright position, it means the plough must be used and it is time to start planting the fields. In contrast, when the plough is seen low in the west in the upside-down position, it means the end of harvesting season [khairuddin2021austronesian].

constellation_description: It marks the direction of the south.

place: Three Carib villages (Konomerume, Karawasinde, and Kubarimye) in Suriname
culture_description: The Kalina or Caribs are indigenous people in the northern coastal areas of South America. They lacked written language before the arrival of Europeans. 53 Carib stars and constellations were known between the 16th century and 1980, with many myths attached to them. Many of these are now unknown: only 20 stars and constellations (Table 2 in the source) remain both known and identified (have been drawn in the sky by villagers and referred to Western sky-maps). The drawings of the first 3 constellations are missing from the source for lack of reproduction rights, and the original sources (1931, 1943) could not be found. These missing constellations are: (001) Barbecue of the Sucker Catfish (Pakamu Sura-ri) which corresponds to Corvus, (002) Four-Eyed Fish (Kutaiyuman) which corresponds to Scorpio and Sagitta, and (003) without English gloss (Pakamuyuman) which also corresponds to Scorpio. This sky culture has 8 constellation line figures, one constellation without lines, and names for the Pleiades, the Milky Way, alf CMa (Sirius), alf Vir (Spica), and Venus. Two other constellations are only imprecisely identified and could not be included: Scorpio (Sibirisiyuman) as Scorpio, and Barbecue (Siritjo sura-ri) as unknown parts of Pegasus and of Andromeda.
certainty: Eliminated one unnamed star between bet Per and chi Cas (which best matched M34, a galaxy) and two unidentified stars at the end of the tail.

certainty: Two faint stars at the end of lines (sig Her, i Boo A) are guesses, but match well in shape. Another star in drawn isolated inside the body, as an eye, but could not be identified, so is not added. No link is drawn in the source between rho Boo and sig Boo (the start of the tail) likely because they're too close together. A link appears logical and is added here, to close the polygon of the body.

constellation_description: These represent the three stones surrounding the indoor cooking fire. The stars enclose nebula M42, which is seen as a smoky fire. This Mayan asterism is in their creation story Popol Vuh, and is still recognized now.

place: Guatemala: Santa Lucía Utatlán, Momostenango, and others
certainty: Lining not certain: this follows the ladle semantic.
constellation_description: The ancient name for Big Dipper is 'Seven Macaw' (native: Vucub Caquix), found in some K'iche' dictionaries. In the Mayan creation story, Popol Vuh, this is a bird deity who climbs up a tree to proclaim himself the Sun, but is shot down by Hunahpu. Also called 'Bird', and the name of the bird incorporates the number seven.

place: Guatemala: Santa Lucía Utatlán, Momostenango, and others

constellation_description: The name means 'ladle' or 'spoon'.

place: Ten Kolam villages (south of Nagpur, India): Dubaguda, Janguguda, Jamini (Duttaguda), Laxmipur, Sonapur, Landgi Pod, Chinchpod, Bhimnala, Raipur (Khadki), Kakban
culture_description: The Kolams (also called the Kolowars) are an ancient tribe mostly spread in south Central India, speaking a Dravidian language, co-located with the Gond and Banjara tribes. The population size is around 400,000. They were foragers until 1940-1950, and practised slash-and-burn cultivation using hoes and digging sticks; at the time of documentation they were agricultural. They have historically (1200 AD) served as priests for another co-located tribe, the Gonds (but are genetically distinct). The astronomy is extensive, if overly generalised: they do not seem to record changes in the sky over the year, and they recall the sky only during the pre-monsoon period when it is well observed. They borrow traditions from the Gonds, who retained more detailed descriptions of some of these common constellations. Besides the 7 lined constellations, three others are unidentified. One is named in two villages, but remains unidentified: the Danedare Pila, a spiral-shaped asterism (either the tail of Scorpius, or a counter-clockwise spiral of stars that also looks like a snake about 10 deg. east of Scorpius). Another constellation in Leo is also named in two villages, having been borrowed from the Gond tribe, but is not well identified by the Kolams: Murda (a dead body). Also, in Taurus is (in five villages) Bhori, a bird (Aldebaran) with two unidentified eggs.
english_names: Golden Cot Pursued by Three Thieves
certainty: The three thieves are disconnected, as per description and an explicit drawing on slides by the authors (slides not public). This is unlike the variants of other tribes where the thieves are not individually named, so are lined into a procession. There are alternative semantics, since this is a group scene (four objects: a cot and three humans).
constellation_description: A cot (called Mandater) which three unconnected stars (a Kolam, a Gond, and a Pardhan chief, in order starting from the cot) are trying to steal. In only one village, there is an alternative semantic: a container with four edges; in early June, at sunset it is close to zenith and upside down, so pours out the rains (the remaining three stars are on their way to drink the water).

certainty: The semantic is multidimensional: the plough semantic is recent and clear, but there is an additional (probably older and vague) hunting-related semantic. It also is confused for the line of 3 stars in Cygnus, CON Kolam 018. This version is the one the source appears to prefer.
constellation_description: Tivpate or Tipan (an instrument used to sow seeds) is known in almost all Kolam villages, although in two villages this plough is the sword of Orion, while the belt has the semantics of CON Kolam 018 (a tower of three pots). It is also known as the belt of a hunter, and the region around the Orion Nebula as a sword. (This may be a weak memory of the original Hindu myth which sees the entire Orion as a deer and the three stars of the belt as an arrow.) In one village, the entire Orion is included, not just the belt (but this structure is not identified). In another village, the region around Orion is a farming scene (like that around Sirius, alf CMa) with Medi, Kalave and Tandel or Aidal (a bull).

certainty: There are alternative semantics, since this is a group scene (three objects: a tree and two humans).
constellation_description: The only southern constellation recognised. Besides the tree itself, alf Cen is Murta (an old lady) and bet Cen is Kadma (a young lady), both there to collect flowers (they are included, unconnected). In one village, these two stars are instead called Oedda (man) and Pilla (woman). In one village, other neighbouring small stars are flowers of Mahua (Irup pokke) --- these are not identified so not included.

certainty: Slightly uncertain. The source states that Samdur may also be in Centaurus.
constellation_description: Samdur is the Great Square of Pegasus. The name means both the sea (badly pronounced) and an object where all points are equidistant. Five different animals are said to come to drink and feed there: Namli (a peacock), Barre (a buffalo), Pande (a frog), Mekam (a deer), and Gurram (a horse). The source suggests that the stars in the northeast in Pegasus are the deer, Cassiopeia the peacock, Aquila the horse, Cygnus the buffalo, and Pisces the frog.

certainty: Uncertain in composition. The source says that they probably include some stars above the conventional Scorpius in this constellation, but these are not identified. certainty: The stars in Cyg are not identified, but the authors (on slides) draw the pots over the Cyg backbone, so the 3 brightest backbone stars are chosen.

place: 12 Korku villages in Northern Maharashtra, about 200 km north of Amravati: Zingapur, Rani Tamboli, Kawadaziri, Makhala, Raipur, Borata Kheda, Bela, Jamunala, Baghalinga, Gaulkheda Bazar, Hatida, Gadgabhandum
culture_description: The Korku (population over 1 million) are an ancient Indian tribe, descendants of Austro-Asian settlers (believed the first modern humans to settle in India). They live in central India, in sparsely populated forest reserves, and traditional villages. Despite being geographically and culturally isolated from the mainstream Indian population, and having little contact with other tribes, their traditional lifestyle is changing, and traditional knowledge is being lost. Originally hunter-gatherers, within the last 50 years they were forced to practice subsistence agriculture. All 12 villages recognise the Big Dipper as a Golden Cot Pursued by Three Thieves, and a plough in Orion's belt. The other sky objects are less frequently named. Six constellations have semantics which imply lines (a rectangular cot, a circular ground, a well, a dagger, etc.), but there are more named stars and star groups. Other sky regions are mentioned, but the star groups or constellations are not clearly nor consistently identified, so are not included: in IAU Scorpius and Corona Australis, one village identifies a scorpion in the Sco-CrA region, with the Western Sco as its fang, other villages identify the Western Scorpius (or only its tail) as the true Scorpius, and another village identifies a scorpion below the Western Sco. There are also more variants of the names among villages than included here. (Note: There are inconsistencies in [vahia2016korku] between Table 2 containing names and the preceding text; Table 2 is taken as the leading source in star identification, which is more precise there, but the names are corrected according to the text.)
english_names: Golden Cot Pursued by Three Thieves
certainty: The three thieves are disconnected, because they are individually named.
constellation_description: The 4 stars are a cot (Sona Parkom) made of gold. The bed is deformed (not a perfect rectangle) since it is being pulled by one of the thieves. The thieves are individually named: eps UMa is thief 1 (Pahila Chor), zet01 UMa is thief 2 (Dusara Chor), and eta UMa is thief 3 (Tisara Chor. In 5 villages, thief 2 carries a water jug (g UMa, Alcor). In 4 villages, this constellation is used as a clock in the night.

constellation_description: Orion's belt is Harnangar (a plough with 3 blades), bet and kap Ori are Doba 1 and 2 (bullocks), and alf Ori is Nangarnara manus (the man ploughing the field).

constellation_description: The circular ground for thrashing rice husk (Khiryan) is alf to gam Tau. The rope to control the bulls (Michan) is eta Ori. The central pole (Miryan) is tet02 Tau.

constellation_description: The Mahua tree is a remarkably multifunctional native tree, highly prized by the Korku, and cultivated both on the plains and in the forest areas. It is thus surprising that this constellation is only reported by one village.

culture_description: The Koyukon are a Subarctic tribe in the Yukon river valley in central Alaska, speaking a language from the Athapaskan family. Subarctic tribes do not have extensive astronomical lore. There are two sets of sources. (1) In [nelson1983make,miller1997stars], only one constellation is documented and drawn, the Big Dipper (with a closed bowl), but with a different meaning. The Koyukon also marked the movement of alf Aql (Altair), Cassiopeia, the Pleiades, and Orion's belt to keep track of time, but these sky objects are not named or identified [miller1997stars] (page 84). The source [miller1997stars] attributes the information to [nelson1983make], whose author lived with the Koyukon for 16 months in 1976-1977. (2) In recent fieldwork [cannon2021northerndene] with Northern Dene peoples (2010-2021), a characteristic principal constellation was documented in Allakaket, Alaska: the incarnated spirit of a Traveler figure who circled the world in Distant Time. Also here were documented names for the Milky Way, alf Aql (Altair), and gam Aql (Tarazed), as morning stars.
certainty: Certain.
constellation_description: The Koyukon call the Big Dipper a man. According to legend, he argues fiercely with Raven and leaves for the sky, where he becomes Nosikghaltaala, a 'measurer of time'. The tip of the handle is his head, the rest of the handle is his back, and the two pointer stars of the bowl are his buttocks. It serves as the tribe's primary clock.

certainty: Most stars are identified, and a pictograph is drawn in the source [cannon2021northerndene]. The line figure here follows the star path of each body part listed, connecting them. pi2 UMa is not named in the text, but is in the drawing (so is included here). The head (31 Lyn) is linked here to the body, since a star is identified as the neck.
constellation_description: A large man-animal constellation composed of 15 groups of stars named as body parts. Like in the Sahtúot’įnę culture, most of the stars are extremely dim. The figure is much smaller than whole-sky: approximately 66 degrees at its greatest width between its left and right hands. It is centered on the Big Dipper as either the man's right leg (native: nełneyhts'ene' betl'ene') or his tail (native: bekaa). The dim star (31 Lyn) that composes the head is located near the heart of the Gwich'in, Ahtna, Upper Tanana, and Lower Tanana whole-sky constellations.

culture_description: The Lower Tanana sky culture is the result of fieldwork with Northern Dene peoples, investigating how they conceptualize and integrate the sky into systems of knowledge, practices, cosmology, and spirituality. Characteristic is a principal constellation: the incarnated spirit of a Traveler figure who circled the world in Distant Time. An additional unidentified constellation represents a malevolent being.
english_names: The One Who Continuously Walks; The One Who Started Paddling Among Things; Big Sky
certainty: Most stars are identified, and a pictograph is drawn in the source [cannon2021northerndene]. The line figure here follows the star path of each body part listed, connecting them.
constellation_description: A whole-sky constellation associated with the Traveler, spans approximately 117 degrees and is composed of at least 25 groups of stars, of which 22 are named body parts. A man-animal figure,  Nogheyoli's heart (native: bedraya') (21 Lyn) is the embodiment of the things that the Traveler worked with while transforming the world. He wears a backpack (native: beghala’) and holds a spear/walking stick/medicine staff (native: bedechena') in his left hand. The constellation is remarkable for its details: five dim stars outline his hair (native: begho'); his right hand is divided into smaller groups of stars for his palm (native: belok'a), fingers (native: belots'ula'), and thumb (native: belochetth); his left foot is composed to two smaller groups that distinguish his toes (native: bekalats'ula') and heel (native: bekatwtl). Nogheyoli is in a defensive posture overhead. His right arm and hand are extended to stave off a malevolent being (an unidentified constellation, as in the Gwich'in sky culture). The toes and heel of his left foot are splayed out like talons, while his right leg and foot appear human. His left hand grips his spear, with distinct thumb, fingers, and wrist. His ears are standing upright like that of an alert animal. Big Dipper is his tail (native: becha'). His heart (21 Lyn) is unconnected. The same for the 5 stars of his hair (psi Aur) and his eyes (iot Aur, bet Tau).

place: Madura Island, off the northeastern coast of Java, Indonesia
culture_description: The Madurese have a maritime tradition. Like the Bugis and the Mandar, this probably originated with the Austronesians, who brought an advanced maritime technology with them when migrating to Java and Madura. There are still many fishing villages along the coast of Madura; some of the fishermen still use the stars and asterisms for navigation (alongside modern technology), while in other villages the fishermen still have the knowledge but do not use it any more. Some of the younger fishermen lost this traditional knowledge. The prefix bintang (star) is added to almost all stellar objects. They used the term Bintang tera' (Bright stars) when navigating to the north, with Bintang tera' bhiruh (Blue bright star) recognized as Vega. They also used the asterism Bintang Sekoh (Elbow stars), i.e., the Western Summer triangle (Vega–Altair–Deneb). When navigating to the south, they used Tara laok (alf and bet Cen). Their most popular asterism was Bintang Pe'-Kope'an, a kind of Madurese fish (the constellation Crux). For Madurese farmers, the most popular asterism was Bintang Nangghala (Plough), the central part of Orion. The Madurese culture leans heavily on the Javanese culture, especially wrt farming. Both cultures use the Pleiades and Orion as signs of farming time, and the names of stars and asterisms are quite similar, such as the Plough.
english_names: Two Stars in the South; Two Stars
native_names: Bintang Tara Laok; Bintang Jejer Duwe'
constellation_description: Point to the south direction.

culture_description: Like the Bugis and Madura (who are also migrated Malays), the Malay culture is a mix of sea- and land usage, with sea traditions, but current farming and fishing practices. The field work attempted to link the local constellations to specific ecological adaptations, and from there, to certain economic lifestyles. Four constellations were identified: 1) Belantik (Orion), 2) Bintang Tujuh (Pleiades), 3) Pari (Crux), and 4) Biduk (Big Dipper). The first two constellations are normally associated with land-based ecological niches, whereas the latter two constellations are usually linked to sea-based ecological adaptations.
certainty: The exact below-belt stars are not identified. A spring-spear trap seems to have at least two connected linear elements [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Springing_branch_spear_trap.JPG]: a spear and a spring, rope, or trip wire. I thus draw it as two linear segments: the belt plus bet Ori (Rigel), connected at an angle. A conservative asterism would be only the belt, but the source says otherwise.
constellation_description: Orion's belt and below. Symbolizes a spring-spear trap that can be used to kill land animals of any size, from as big as an elephant to as small as a porcupine.

constellation_description: Used as a marker for the southern direction in the past. Current people only recognize this constellation as pointing towards the south in a general manner: they do not use the (missing) tail of this constellation as a more precise direction of the south.

constellation_description: Used to locate the north direction. The five stars that make up the long handles of both Big and Little Dipper are mapped out as the Biduk, a small paddle boat used for catching fish or ferrying goods on the river and coastal areas. Its keel allows it to cut across the waves, thus it is a sea-worthy vessel unlike other riverine vessels. This type of boat has a tall front, low back, and sometimes the paddler is standing. This may explain the shape of the figure.

culture_description: The Mandar are an ethnic group on the island of Sulawesi in Indonesia, with a strong seafaring and trading tradition. The sailors used stars and asterisms to pinpoint the cardinal directions. This sky culture has 6 constellation line figures, explicitly drawn with stars identified, so can be considered certain unless specified otherwise. The source also provides names for 3 other identified sky objects (the Pleiades, the Milky Way, and Venus), and the name of an unidentified sky object: Star of Fishermen or Bittoéng Pambosei, which is a marker towards the north and usually appears in February, during the northwest monsoon season. These constellations reflect the seafaring tradition. The Bugis sky culture is related (they are neighbouring ethnic groups on the island of Sulawesi) so it is similar.
constellation_description: Is used as an indicator for the south. Five stars (including Crux) form a house with uneven foundations.

culture_description: The Maricopa live in south-central Arizona, in the Southwest. The information is attributed to the anthropologist Leslie Spier and dated 1929-1930 (exact reference unclear). Only 4 constellations have lines; another (Mountain Sheep) is without lines. There are other constellations documented, such as Earth Doctor (2 stars in Scorpius) and Scorpion (other stars in Scorpius), but the exact stars have not been identified. Other constellations are identified with modern ones but without confirming any lines (they are not included here). The constellations represent or refer to myth characters: Coyote, Cold, Spider, Fox, Earth Doctor.
constellation_description: Coyote is the Big Dipper, and the net extends to UMi. Coyote holds the net at one end. When he hears fish jumping, he turns around, pulling the net behind him and making a bend in it (the slight bend in the Big Dipper handle).

constellation_description: The three stars are an antelope, a mountain sheep, and a deer. There is also an arrow going through, but not well identified. The Mountain Sheep Pursuer (Sirius) is stalking and has shot the mountain sheep.

culture_description: The Mi'kmaq live in the forests and on the shores of Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island, eastern New Brunswick, and the Gaspé Peninsula, in NE North America. Because of the short growing season and the abundance of land and water wildlife, they hunted and fished rather than farmed---and this reflects in their sky myths. Their culture hero is a character called Gluskap (spelled also Glooscap), who showed the people how to hunt and fish, and named the stars and constellations. The source [miller1997stars] covers the Mi'kmaq (spelled Micmac) on pages 36-40, based on the early ethnographic paper [hagar1900celestial], which in turn references a Le Clerq (1691) to confirm part of the information. The most salient feature of the Mi'kmaq sky is the interpretation of the Big Dipper rectangle as a celestial bear, and its handle plus stars in IAU Boo as seven bird hunters. Though the bear was known to many widely separated tribes, the seven hunters in UMa and Boo appear peculiar to the Mi'kmaq and the Iroquois (elsewhere the hunter group seems to be limited to the stars of the Big Dipper.) Acc. to [hagar1900celestial], the common origin of the legend is clear for the Mi'kmaq and the Iroquois, who have been in frequent contact. The Cherokees also knew the three (and sometimes seven) hunters who pursue a bear. The stars, native names, gloss, and myth are precisely identified for CON Mikmaq 001-003 (004 is also documented but not identified in the sky) in [hagar1900celestial]. Line figures are then drawn explicitly in [miller1997stars].
constellation_description: The bear (CON Mikmaq 002) is said to emerge from her den in the spring, and her spirit goes back into its den in the fall.

Acc. to [hagar1900celestial], the common origin of the legend is clear for the Mi'kmaq and the Iroquois, who have been in frequent contact. The Cherokees also knew the three (and sometimes seven) hunters who pursue a bear. The stars, native names, gloss, and myth are precisely identified for CON Mikmaq 001-003 (004 is also documented but not identified in the sky) in [hagar1900celestial]. constellation_description: The bear is said to emerge from her den (CON Mikmaq 001) in the spring. During the summer, when she stands upright, seven hunters (CON Mikmaq 003) chase her. In the fall, with the bear lying on its back preparing for hibernation, several of the hunters drop out of the chase (below the horizon). The remaining hunters kill the bear. Through the winter, her skeleton lies upon its back in the sky, but her life spirit enters another bear who lies upon her back in the den, invisible and hibernating. The story repeats the next spring, and eternally.

Because of the short growing season and the abundance of land and water wildlife, they hunted and fished rather than farmed---and this reflects in their sky myths. constellation_description: The hunters represent birds, individually named. They chase the bear (CON Mikmaq 002). The second hunger carries a pot (Alcor, g UMa), so that, when the bear is killed, he may cook the meat. In the fall, several of the hunters drop out of the chase (below the horizon). The remaining hunters kill the bear. Blood splatters the robin (eps UMa), explaining the spot on its breast.

constellation_description: This is a second hunting scene in the sky. Acc. [hagar1900celestial], one Mi'kmaq remembered that his tribe once thought there was another bear hidden under the sky near the pole, and that the neighbouring stars were hunters circling around in a vain endeavour to locate its den. Both bears are mentioned in Le Clerq (1691): the Great and Little Bear, this being the earliest reference to these star groups.

period: 1999-2009 fieldwork, plus early 20th-century (1921) fieldwork by R. Lehmann-Nitsche, which continue to be the main source on the astronomy of indigenous people of the Guaycurú linguistic group
place: communities centered around Colonia Cacique Catán, and other communities in the region such as Colonia Aborigen Chaco and Colonia Aborigen El Pastoril (Chaco Province, Argentina)
culture_description: The Mocoví (or Moqoit) are an indigenous people of the Gran Chaco in South America. The present-day Mocoví population consists of 18000 people, most in the Argentine provinces of Chaco and Santa Fe. The Mocoví language belongs to the Guaycurú linguistic group, as do the Toba, Abipones, Pilagás and Caduveos. Before coming under the influence of missionaries, they were migratory hunters (the favored species of game animals were deer, peccaries, and rheas), fishers, and gatherers. Today, the Mocoví have become assimilated, their culture has undergone substantial change, and only a minority have retained their native language. They had and still have a great practical knowledge of the night sky, for orientation and time-telling. The celestial plane, one of the three planes of the Mocoví cosmos, is called Piguim. This is a cold region, where atmospheric phenomena (winds, rain, thunder, lightning) belong. The celestial plane is hostile to humans, but is inhabited by powerful beings who look like the Mocoví but belong to mythical time, owners of astronomical and atmospheric phenomena. The vast majority of these celestial inhabitants are women: the celestial (and many Mocoví constellations) are associated with abundance, since almost all of the water in this region of Chaco is rain water. Most of the Mocoví asterisms are within or near the Milky Way, which acts as an articulating axis of the Mocoví cosmos. There are 12 identified constellations (of which 8 lined), and names for many sky objects. The source identifies stars and draws some line figures. Another Corral of the Dog (native: Qo'parit pioq) (similar to CON Mocovi 001) has not been identified.
constellation_description: This is a fence encircling a ranch.

Today, the Mocoví have become assimilated, their culture has undergone substantial change, and only a minority have retained their native language. They had and still have a great practical knowledge of the night sky, for orientation and time-telling. The celestial plane, one of the three planes of the Mocoví cosmos, is called Piguim. This is a cold region, where atmospheric phenomena (winds, rain, thunder, lightning) belong. The celestial plane is hostile to humans, but is inhabited by powerful beings who look like the Mocoví but belong to mythical time, owners of astronomical and atmospheric phenomena. The vast majority of these celestial inhabitants are women: the celestial (and many Mocoví constellations) are associated with abundance, since almost all of the water in this region of Chaco is rain water. Most of the Mocoví asterisms are within or near the Milky Way, which acts as an articulating axis of the Mocoví cosmos. There are 12 identified constellations (of which 8 lined), and names for many sky objects. The source identifies stars and draws some line figures. Another Corral of the Dog (native: Qo'parit pioq) (similar to CON Mocovi 001) has not been identified.
constellation_description: This reflects a Christian tradition implanted in the Chaco, in the communities linked to the Santuario de la Virgen de la Laguna. These asterisms are three triangle-shaped variants that reproduce the profile of the Virgin with her mantle.

place: 9 villages on Nicobar Islands: Camorta Island (village: Munak), Nancowry Island (Kakana, Pilpillow), Chowra Island (Taeela), Teressa Island (Bengali, Kalasi, Minyuk, Luxi, Enam)
culture_description: The Nicobar Islands are an archipelagic island chain in the eastern Indian Ocean, north of Indonesia, inhabited by the Nicobarese tribe (of Austroasiatic origin). A significant fraction of the elder population was washed away in a tsunami in 2004; the rest have been resettled. This perspective of the skies was documented from the survivors. Their astronomy was used for navigation in the past (now, these navigation skills are lost). The constellations identified are located in the Western Big Dipper, Crux, the belt of Orion, and Scorpius (each recognised in 4-7 villages). Scorpius (called Miroleach or Crab on Teressa Island) is not identified except as the Western sky region of IAU Sco. An earlier (1997) source states that the Nicobarese are aware of Centaurus (Onchiana), but this constellation is not identified in [vahia2018nicobars]. The IAU constellations Lepus and Lupus are confused in [vahia2018nicobars] (probably a typo, 'Lupus: Position of Lepus is used for navigation'); neither is included here, since not identifiable. Names are documented for Mars and Venus, the Milky Way, Pleiades, Polaris, and Aldebaran. The source does not provide drawings of these native constellations (only textual descriptions), but these constellations were identified by the natives using modern charts, so lines are drawn here if the textual description is sufficiently precise to infer a shape (for example, 4 stars in Big Dipper make a box, and 3 stars a ladder). The source also refers to charts from an older source, which are less well identifiable.
certainty: The line figure is drawn disconnected into its 2 components (box and ladder), since the descriptions appear to consider them separate objects in a scene. This constellation is documented in 7 villages.
constellation_description: In Pilpillow village on Nancowry Island, the first 4 stars (Ungyanga) form the basket to keeps the hens safe and, the trailing 3 stars form the ladder to climb to the basket. In Taeela village on Chowra Island, this constellation is called Honrulohotam; the 4 stars (Lenob) are a basket to keep hens, and the 3 trailing stars make a ladder. Similar descriptions and names from Kalasi, Minyuk, and Bengali villages on Teressa Island. In Munak village on Camorta Island, the 4 leading stars (Uyav) are the coconut tree, and the trailing 3 (Inoat or Dav) are the tool to cut coconut from the tree.

place: 14 Pardhi villages in Central India, near the city of Amravati: Saur, Hartala, Hiwara BK, Wadura Beda, Daryapur, Darapur, Shinganapur, Khairi Donoda, Mangarul Chavhala Beda, Mukinpur Beda, Ajanti Beda, Zombadi Beda, Jagatpur Beda, Wadgaon
culture_description: The Pardhis have a small population (50,000) scattered over a large territory centered on Amravati. The genetic evidence points to the Pardhi evolving from the original Austro-Asiatic settlers (believed the first modern humans to settle in India), but they now speak newer Indo-European languages. The Pardhi were classified as criminals by the British during the colonial era, resulting in poor development: they still live in isolated, economically disadvantaged areas, and they often scavenge. (Of the 14 villages visited, in 3 the villages were cultivators, and in the others hunters and gatherers.) This provides them with a unique perspective of the sky, related to hunting and gathering. While they do have some beliefs in common of other tribes, there is a degree of originality due to their long traditions. The imagery is animistic: that of plants, animals and birds, far more so than for any other Indian tribe (as is expected if the ancestral population was only dependent on hunting and gathering for survival). The Pardhi association of Orion with deer and Taurus with a bird trap are unique. There are four constellations with lines, of which the Cobra or Snake in Scorpius is uncertain in star composition. The star pair called Two Bird's Eggs is uncertain in star composition, and has two variants. Another constellation, The Hilagoi Bird in the Pleiades-Taurus-Auriga region, remains unidentified.
english_names: Cot of the Old Lady Pursued by Three Thieves
certainty: The three thieves are disconnected, because they are individually named.
constellation_description: The rectangular cot of the old lady is called Budinu Khatalu. The three stars are called (in 6 villages) Three Thieves (Taran chor); in only 1 village, they are called Three Dead Men (Melela manas), and in another village Three Birds (individually named by their species: eps UMa is Teetar, zet UMa is Bater, and eta UMa is Lawada). In 2 other villages, they are three people from the Pardhi tribe, individually named: eps UMa is Chavandeyo (a person from the Gaon Pardhi tribe with the surname Chauhan who worshipped the goddess Chavanda), zet UMa is Khodyaryo or Pimplajyo (a person from the Gaon Pardhi tribe with the surname Solanke who worshipped the goddess Pimpalaj or Khodyar Devi), and eta UMa is Korobyo (a person from the Gaon Pardhi tribe with the surname Pawar who worshipped the goddess Korobyo).

culture_description: This culture is the result of ethnographic collection between 1896 and 1907 (the time of writing of the original source, [otescu1907]). The documentation of these 39 constellations was done by village teachers speaking to elderly locals, using sky charts with the standard Western constellations already marked. Line figures were collected for all constellations except the Pleiades cluster. The English translation which is available for [otescu1907] retains the detailed explanations for the constellations, but does not retain the original charts: it instead draws a simplified modern version excluding some minor stars. For the line figures, the leading source is then [crtro] unless otherwise specified. [crtro] is a modern Romanian digital exhibition based on the same [otescu1907]; this has detailed, individually drawn line figures over modern sky charts. Two constellations in Tau are identical, but with different names and semantics. Many stories describing these constellations are local, related to historical, agricultural, and religious traditions in Romania. Others are preserved from the Greek traditions.
certainty: This only includes the Big Dipper asterism, not the larger UMa.
constellation_description: It is said that the Emperor Trajan transported the Dacian prisoners to Rome with the Great Chariot, and the Dacian captains with the Little Chariot. There is also a Romanian legend explaining that God made this chariot move backwards to humble the charioteer.

For the line figures, the leading source is then [crtro] unless otherwise specified. [crtro] is a modern Romanian digital exhibition based on the same [otescu1907]; this has detailed, individually drawn line figures over modern sky charts. Two constellations in Tau are identical, but with different names and semantics. Many stories describing these constellations are local, related to historical, agricultural, and religious traditions in Romania. Others are preserved from the Greek traditions.
constellation_description: In a Romanian sky legend, the axe is used by a charioteer to smash the head of a dead person which was thrown in front of him by the devil. The alternative name (Devil's Chariot) links to beliefs that this chariot used to transport the soil of the sinners to hell.

culture_description: Alexandre Ruelle was an astronomy professor at the Paris Observatory. In 1786, he drew a star chart for the general public (in the service of education), titled 'Nouvelle Uranographie ou méthode très facile pour apprendre les constellations' [Ruelle1786]. This shows 70 constellation line figures for the entire sky. This was a time when the classical Greek constellations were only represented in the traditional style, using allegorical pictographs, and not line figures. Ruelle's approach was very modern: he eliminated the pictographs, and drew simple lines which linked stars. His constellation lines, although different from the modern Western ones, are just as complex: they draw large geometric shapes linking both principal and secondary stars. Ruelle's star chart has historical importance: it is the earliest attempt (in the Western tradition) at reconstructing the classical Greek constellations with geometrical line figures, apparently without trying to mimic any pictograph. There is a single predecessor: in 1625, Jacob van der Heyden, a publisher, painter, sculptor, and engraver, published 'Globus Concavus, Et Quidem Coelestis Stellatus': a celestial globe with faint line figures, which are difficult to see. Ruelle’s innovation itself was not disseminated widely, and did not gain broad acceptance in his time: only in late 19th century, with the continued development of Western astronomy, lined constellation figures like Ruelle’s became commonplace. The source has two polar charts (covering declinations between each pole and the equator) and a partial equatorial chart (covering declinations between the equator and 35 deg. north and south). For some constellations, there are differences in lines between the charts (when both are clear, two variants are taken: the polar version is leading, and the equatorial variant has '_eq' added to the identifier). Occasionally, Ruelle also draws lines to galaxies (for example, M81, M82 are linked into Ursa Major); they are not included here. A small number of stars couldn't be identified (the stars are imprecisely positioned on the chart, and some are not labelled), so they are also not included. There appear to be some typos in the star names: the stars are identified here such that they match the drawings best. Obsolete constellations appear in Ruelle's chart: Musca Borealis (La Mouche), Cerberus (Cerbere), Argo Navis (Le Vaisseau), Antinoüs.

In 1786, he drew a star chart for the general public (in the service of education), titled 'Nouvelle Uranographie ou méthode très facile pour apprendre les constellations' [Ruelle1786]. In 1786, he drew a star chart for the general public (in the service of education), titled 'Nouvelle Uranographie ou méthode très facile pour apprendre les constellations' [Ruelle1786]. This shows 70 constellation line figures for the entire sky. This was a time when the classical Greek constellations were only represented in the traditional style, using allegorical pictographs, and not line figures. Ruelle's approach was very modern: he eliminated the pictographs, and drew simple lines which linked stars. His constellation lines, although different from the modern Western ones, are just as complex: they draw large geometric shapes linking both principal and secondary stars. Ruelle's star chart has historical importance: it is the earliest attempt (in the Western tradition) at reconstructing the classical Greek constellations with geometrical line figures, apparently without trying to mimic any pictograph. There is a single predecessor: in 1625, Jacob van der Heyden, a publisher, painter, sculptor, and engraver, published 'Globus Concavus, Et Quidem Coelestis Stellatus': a celestial globe with faint line figures, which are difficult to see. Ruelle’s innovation itself was not disseminated widely, and did not gain broad acceptance in his time: only in late 19th century, with the continued development of Western astronomy, lined constellation figures like Ruelle’s became commonplace. The source has two polar charts (covering declinations between each pole and the equator) and a partial equatorial chart (covering declinations between the equator and 35 deg. north and south). For some constellations, there are differences in lines between the charts (when both are clear, two variants are taken: the polar version is leading, and the equatorial variant has '_eq' added to the identifier). Occasionally, Ruelle also draws lines to galaxies (for example, M81, M82 are linked into Ursa Major); they are not included here. A small number of stars couldn't be identified (the stars are imprecisely positioned on the chart, and some are not labelled), so they are also not included. There appear to be some typos in the star names: the stars are identified here such that they match the drawings best. Obsolete constellations appear in Ruelle's chart: Musca Borealis (La Mouche), Cerberus (Cerbere), Argo Navis (Le Vaisseau), Antinoüs.

The Sahtúot’įnę sky culture is the result of fieldwork with Northern Dene peoples, investigating how they conceptualize and integrate the sky into systems of knowledge, practices, cosmology, and spirituality. Characteristic is a principal constellation: the incarnated spirit of a Traveler figure who circled the world in Distant Time. An additional constellation is unidentified: a malevolent lion/tiger.
english_names: The One Who Went Around the World
certainty: Most stars are identified, and a pictograph is drawn in the source [cannon2021northerndene]. The line figure here follows the star path of each body part listed, connecting them. His right shoulder star is not identified, although it is drawn; it is identified here as 36 UMa based on the drawing.
constellation_description: Yámǫréya is said to have invented Sahtúot’įnę technology, transformed flora and fauna, and shaped the landscape. His spirit incarnated in the stars as a protector, teacher, gamekeeper, and source of knowledge and power. He is composed of at least 11 groups of stars named as body parts. His heart (lam UMa) is not connected. The Big Dipper is his container or cooking vessel identified as Yı́hda or lıbóchə́. Aside from the Big Dipper, the stars are relatively inconspicuous, suggesting a specialized domain of knowledge. In comparison to the Gwich'in and Ahtna whole-sky constellations, Yámǫréya is less than half their size, spanning just over 64 degrees.

period: documentation from older ethnographic sources dated 1916-1968, in Tewa-speaking villages
place: Tewa-speaking Pueblos in New Mexico: San Ildefonso, Isleta, Jemez, Cochiti, Taos, Nambe
certainty: The line figure is drawn in the source (except for the shorter lines between close or small stars) but the shape is not justified. It is a naturalistic human shape with a sash, as the character is named.
constellation_description: The Pueblo myth of Long Sash parallels the biblical story of Moses leading the Israelites out of Egypt. Long Sash was a warrior entrusted by the tribe with the task of leading them to a new land where they could live in peace away from destructive neighbouring tribes. The long journey followed The Endless Trail (the Milky Way), and important points in the journey were marked by The Place of Decision (002), The Place of Doubt (003), and The Three Stars of Helpfulness (004).

period: documentation from older ethnographic sources dated 1916-1968, in Tewa-speaking villages
place: Tewa-speaking Pueblos in New Mexico: San Ildefonso, Isleta, Jemez, Cochiti, Taos, Nambe
certainty: The line figure is drawn in the source, and the two stars are identified.
constellation_description: A step of the journey led by Long Sash (001) where the tribe took the decision to continue. It is said that even today, people look at these stars when making important decisions.

certainty: The line figure is drawn in the source and matches Rey's naturalistic crab. However, the reason for this shape of the line figure is not explained in the source, so is probably a default to a Western figure.
constellation_description: A step of the journey led by Long Sash (001) where Long Sash was doubtful and unsure which way to go; he asked for (and received) guidance from the above.

period: documentation from older ethnographic sources dated 1916-1968, in Tewa-speaking villages
place: Tewa-speaking Pueblos in New Mexico: San Ildefonso, Isleta, Jemez, Cochiti, Taos, Nambe
certainty: The line figure is drawn in the source and is located in IAU Leo, but the stars are only tentatively identified; they are the brightest in Leo, and they match the shape drawn. The shape represents a travois: an A-shaped frame structure that was used to drag loads over land.
constellation_description: Towards the end of the journey led by Long Sash (001), the tribesmen became kinder to one another; some men made a travois (an A-shaped frame structure that was used to drag loads over land) to carry their own, as well as an old woman's belongings.

period: documentation from older ethnographic sources dated 1916-1968, in Tewa-speaking villages
place: Tewa-speaking Pueblos in New Mexico: San Ildefonso, Isleta, Jemez, Cochiti, Taos, Nambe
certainty: Given the group (7 animals) semantic, this is not a line figure.
constellation_description: In the story of Long Sash (001), seven animals (represented by the Big Dipper) each gave Long Sash and his tribe a sign about where they should settle.

period: documentation from older ethnographic sources dated 1916-1968, in Tewa-speaking villages

certainty: The line figure is not drawn in the source. Since the tail is said to refer to the handle only, it must be attached to the animal, so this represents the animal by closing the rectangle of the dipper.
constellation_description: The tail refers to the handle of the Big Dipper.

culture_description: These are said to be Thai indigenous asterisms, despite early influence from China and India. The imported Indian lunar mansions are also used in Thailand for telling time, but are not included here. There are links to Malay, Java, Bugis, Madura, and Burma asterisms (the turtle and/or plough in Orion). The source interprets a primary source (in Thai) from 2012. Another source for explicitly drawn Thai asterisms was used to clarify some line figures. This was referenced and summarised online by a member of the public [http://archive.astronomerswithoutborders.org/member-reports/285-gam2015/gam-blog/2670-drawing-of-traditional-asterisms-in-thailand.html], is the book 'Tai Baan Doo Dao' (in Thai) by N. Pengkaew (2000), which appears to have some variants for both names and constellations. The Thai sources could not be obtained; only the follow-ups in English, [orchiston2021seasia] and (when the former was not clear) the URL above. Both identify the stars, but draw the constellations with pictographs, not line figures. These are simple enough to transcribe as line figures here following the perimeter of the pictographs.
certainty: The line figure follows the perimeter of the pictograph provided in the source, so is uncertain. The turtle is composed of four stars for the paws, and one for the head. They are lined as a convex hull.
constellation_description: The turtle (CON Thai 001) carries the plough (CON Thai 002) on its back.

culture_description: These are said to be Thai indigenous asterisms, despite early influence from China and India. The imported Indian lunar mansions are also used in Thailand for telling time, but are not included here. There are links to Malay, Java, Bugis, Madura, and Burma asterisms (the turtle and/or plough in Orion). The source interprets a primary source (in Thai) from 2012. culture_description: The Tutchone are a Subarctic tribe in the Yukon plateau in Alaska between the Saint Elias Mountains and the Selwyn Mountains, speaking a language from the Athapaskan family. Subarctic tribes do not have extensive astronomical lore. Only one constellation is documented and drawn, similar to the Big Dipper (with a larger closed bowl), but with a different meaning. The source for this line figure is [miller1997stars] page 83-84. This is attributed to [mcclellan1975myoldpeople], the results of ethnographic research carried out from 1949-51 among three linguistically distinct groups in the southern Yukon Territory (Tlingit, Tagish, and Southern Tutchone).
constellation_description: The Southern Tutchone say that there is a big-headed man in the sky. The man's big head is the Big Dipper's bowl and a star in front of the bowl. His spine is the handle of the Big Dipper. One day, a hunter shot an arrow (Alcor, not included in this constellation) into his spine. Before he died, the big-headed man said he would reappear into the northern sky at night, and so he does.

place: Zinacantán, in the southern Mexican state of Chiapas
constellation_description: This small constellation appears low in the southern sky but is clearly visible especially from the Zinacanteco hamlets located on the edge of the escarpment overlooking the Grijalva Valley to the south. According to some Zinacantecos the Cross (001) is thought to have been the cross on which Christ was crucified (which rose to the sky after his death), and the two Thieves' Crosses (006, 007) on each side of the Southern Cross are the crosses on which the two thieves were crucified.

constellation_description: [lehmann1923matacos] The Great Yulo is a mythological bird, the master of the rain, which has an asterism composed of the Pleiades (head, represented by eta Tau alone --- BUT should be the entire Pleiades), the Hyades (body), gam Ori (heels), and Orion's belt (feet). In another narrative (primary sources 1973-1988), this asterism is formed when some corzuela deer flee to the sky to escape their murderous jaguar grandfather. The deer retreat to safety in the sky forming the Patsezlai, the Pleiades; the deer's mother also climbs to the sky as IAU Cru. This myth shows Wichí kinship rules.

place: Dettah and Ndilǫ, Northwest Territories, Canada
culture_description: The Yellowknives Dene sky culture is the result of fieldwork with Northern Dene peoples, investigating how they conceptualize and integrate the sky into systems of knowledge, practices, cosmology, and spirituality. Characteristic is a principal constellation: the incarnated spirit of a Traveler figure who circled the world in Distant Time.
certainty: Most stars are identified, and a pictograph is drawn in the source [cannon2021northerndene]. The line figure here follows the star path of each body part listed, connecting them. The text lists eta UMa in the right arm, incorrectly; corrected acc. drawing to h UMa.
constellation_description: Humanoid constellation composed of 11 groups of named stars that span 76 degrees across the sky. It is said that an ordinary person shot him with an arrow, and the impact broke his back, which is why he has his hands thrown above his head with fingers splayed out. He kicked his legs and feet into the air, which pushed the sky up to where it is now. His head (ups UMa), the place where he was hit with the arrow (or the arrow itself) (g UMa), and the quiver for arrows (tet Boo) are unconnected.

place: on the Zuni river on the Colorado Plateau
culture_description: The Zuni are Western Pueblos in SW North America, who live on the Zuni river on the Colorado Plateau. They illustrate stars on their ceremonial objects (masks, headdresses, altars, murals). After the sun and moon, the most revered celestial body is the Morning Star, formerly the patron of their warriors and hunters. They also recognise and use both Dippers, Orion, the Pleiades, the Milky Way, the North Star and the Evening Star. Using the moon, Orion, and the Pleiades, they mark the time at night, and mark events in the agricultural cycle (such as the planting of corn). The source [miller1997stars] covers the Zuni on pages 180-184, referencing many sources from the period 1900-1981. Most constellations are poorly or incompletely identified in terms of their stars, and few of the line figures are certain. The Zuni had a sky-wide constellation called Chief of the Night. This is described (in unpublished field notes (1929) by the linguist and ethnologist John P. Harrington) as 'the most majestic constellation ever reported from any tribe of Indians. It is a gigantic human figure, bigger than the whole visible sky. Some part of the body of the Chief of the Night is perpetually visible, guarding the earth throughout the year'. This is similar to the whole-sky figures of the Northern Dene cultures in Alaska (Ahtna, Gwich'in, and others). Little of this constellation has been identified in his notes. Later researchers have made a tentative identification of the heart (CON Zuni 007) and arms (CON Zuni 008-009). In addition to these (partly) identified constellations, there is an unidentified one: Stick Game Ones (presumably located near IAU Lib), which is associated with a game played by the Zuni priesthood to conjure rain.
english_names: The Seven; The Great White Bear of the Seven Stars
certainty: It is not clear which is the main semantic: of the two, the simpler (The Seven or The Seven Ones) is mentioned first, with the longer version (The Great White Bear of the Seven Stars) mentioned in only one known record. It is identified by the source as Big Dipper, but without showing a line figure. This line figure closes the rectangle (since the other N-American bear constellations have a closed polygon) but an unlined star group would also be valid to match the group semantic.

This is described (in unpublished field notes (1929) by the linguist and ethnologist John P. Harrington) as 'the most majestic constellation ever reported from any tribe of Indians. It is a gigantic human figure, bigger than the whole visible sky. Some part of the body of the Chief of the Night is perpetually visible, guarding the earth throughout the year'. This is similar to the whole-sky figures of the Northern Dene cultures in Alaska (Ahtna, Gwich'in, and others). Little of this constellation has been identified in his notes. Later researchers have made a tentative identification of the heart (CON Zuni 007) and arms (CON Zuni 008-009). In addition to these (partly) identified constellations, there is an unidentified one: Stick Game Ones (presumably located near IAU Lib), which is associated with a game played by the Zuni priesthood to conjure rain.

place: on the Zuni river on the Colorado Plateau
culture_description: The Zuni are Western Pueblos in SW North America, who live on the Zuni river on the Colorado Plateau. They illustrate stars on their ceremonial objects (masks, headdresses, altars, murals). After the sun and moon, the most revered celestial body is the Morning Star, formerly the patron of their warriors and hunters. They also recognise and use both Dippers, Orion, the Pleiades, the Milky Way, the North Star and the Evening Star. Using the moon, Orion, and the Pleiades, they mark the time at night, and mark events in the agricultural cycle (such as the planting of corn).
